title: America Retold
gist: A Foreign Policy essay-collection landing page marking the U.S. 250th anniversary, reframing American foreign-policy history through unconventional lenses: how other countries promoted U.S. democracy, how frontier violence and organized crime shaped the world, and what decolonization meant for America's rise.

insights.claim: Foreign Policy's America-at-250 essay series argues U.S. foreign-policy history can be told afresh through how other countries promoted democracy in the United States, how U.S. frontier violence and organized crime shaped the world, and what decolonization meant for the American rise.
insights.speaker: FP Contributors
insights.so_what: A revisionist lens on American power offers context for understanding the historical roots of current U.S. global posture and soft-power debates.
insights.quote: They look at how other countries have promoted democracy in the United States, how U.S. frontier violence and organized crime shaped the world, what decolonization meant for the rise of the United States, and more.
insights.locator: The essays below present U.S. foreign policy in a fresh light

insights.claim: One essay in the series argues organized crime and the Mafia were intertwined with U.S. global influence in the 20th century.
insights.speaker: Ryan Gingeras
insights.so_what: Links between illicit networks and state power are a recurring underappreciated channel in geopolitics worth flagging for historical pattern recognition.
insights.quote: Organized crime and U.S. global influence in the 20th century.

insights.claim: Another essay argues that in trying to restore order after World War II, U.S. leaders drew lessons for rebuilding Europe from the dark chapter of 'Bloody Kansas' and the antebellum order.
insights.speaker: Jay Sexton
insights.so_what: Domestic-to-foreign policy transfer is a framework for understanding how internal U.S. historical experience informs its postwar international order-building.
insights.quote: In trying to restore order after World War II, U.S. leaders drew lessons from a dark chapter in their country's history.
insights.locator: From Bloody Kansas to the Rebuilding of Europe

insights.claim: A third essay contends that other countries and their citizens helped the United States live up to its democratic ideals, applying foreign pressure to advance American freedom.
insights.speaker: Alice Baumgartner
insights.so_what: Inverting the usual democracy-promotion narrative (U.S. as exporter) highlights external influence on U.S. domestic reform, a non-obvious historical angle.
insights.quote: How other countries and their citizens helped the United States live up to its ideals.
